<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Power sums, Bernoulli numbers, and Euler-Maclaurin</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  table.plain td.num{font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;color:var(--cyan)}
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 Faulhaber 2 Bernoulli numbers 3 Zeta values 4 Euler-Maclaurin 5 Functional equation 6 Accurate ζ(s)

Power sums, Bernoulli numbers, and the shadow of $\zeta$

From $1+2+\cdots+n=\tfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ to $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$ to $1+2+3+\cdots=-\tfrac{1}{12}$ — one family of rational numbers, the Bernoulli numbers $B_n$, ties the three together and drives Euler-Maclaurin summation, the functional equation of $\zeta$, and the analytic continuation of every L-function in the notebook.

blocks.html:
1. Faulhaber: sums of $k^m$ as polynomials in $n$

Gauss's schoolboy trick tells you $1+2+\cdots+n=\tfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ — a quadratic in $n$. Less well known, but equally elementary:

$\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} k^2 = \frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6},\qquad \sum_{k=1}^{n} k^3 = \left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\right)^2.$

Each sum is a polynomial in $n$. The pattern, discovered by Jacob Bernoulli (and earlier by Faulhaber in special cases), is that

$\displaystyle S_m(n):=\sum_{k=1}^{n} k^m = \frac{1}{m+1}\sum_{j=0}^{m}\binom{m+1}{j} B_j\, n^{m+1-j},$

where $B_j$ are the Bernoulli numbers (§2). So $S_m$ is a polynomial of degree $m+1$ in $n$ with leading coefficient $\tfrac{1}{m+1}$ — unsurprising, since $S_m(n)$ is the Riemann sum for $\int_0^n x^m\,dx=\tfrac{n^{m+1}}{m+1}$, and the Bernoulli numbers are the discrete correction to the integral.

Why leading $\tfrac{1}{m+1}$? The largest term $k=n$ contributes $n^m$; summing $n$ such terms near the top gives roughly $\int_0^n x^m\,dx=n^{m+1}/(m+1)$. The remaining Bernoulli corrections all have lower degree in $n$.

params.title: Faulhaber polynomial generator
params.hint: pick $m$, compare polynomial vs direct sum

layout.html: Blue bars: direct sum $\sum_{k=1}^{n} k^m$ for each $n$. Yellow curve: Faulhaber polynomial evaluated as a continuous function. They coincide at integer $n$ to machine precision.

blocks.html: Before trusting the machinery, it pays to rediscover two of these sums from data alone — the fingerprint of a polynomial in $n$ is strong enough that a handful of samples pins it down.

config.title: Induce the rule: $1+2+\cdots+n$
config.hint: Five examples, two hidden. Formula uses the variable $n$.

config.title: Induce the rule: $1+2+\cdots+n$
config.hint: Five examples, two hidden. Formula uses the variable $n$.

config.title: Induce the rule: $1^3+2^3+\cdots+n^3$
config.hint: The numbers 1, 9, 36, 100 look familiar — they are the squares of something simpler.

config.title: Induce the rule: $1^3+2^3+\cdots+n^3$
config.hint: The numbers 1, 9, 36, 100 look familiar — they are the squares of something simpler.

Used in
Power sums, Bernoulli numbers, and Euler-Maclaurin · Bernoulli numbers and their generating function

params.title: Faulhaber polynomial generator
params.hint: pick $m$, compare polynomial vs direct sum

layout.html: Blue bars: direct sum $\sum_{k=1}^{n} k^m$ for each $n$. Yellow curve: Faulhaber polynomial evaluated as a continuous function. They coincide at integer $n$ to machine precision.

blocks.html:
2. Bernoulli numbers and their generating function

The Bernoulli numbers $B_n$ are defined by the single generating-function identity

$\displaystyle \frac{t}{e^{t}-1} \;=\; \sum_{n\ge 0} B_n\, \frac{t^n}{n!}.$

Expanding the left side as a power series in $t$ and comparing coefficients gives $B_0=1$, $B_1=-\tfrac12$, $B_2=\tfrac16$, $B_3=0$, $B_4=-\tfrac{1}{30}$, and so on. A key symmetry is visible by rewriting

$\displaystyle \frac{t}{e^{t}-1}+\frac{t}{2} \;=\; \frac{t}{2}\cdot\frac{e^{t}+1}{e^{t}-1} \;=\; \frac{t}{2}\coth(t/2),$

which is an even function of $t$. Subtracting off the $B_1 t/1!=-t/2$ term, all odd powers must vanish: $B_{2k+1}=0$ for $k\ge 1$. The even values alternate in sign and grow faster than any geometric rate.

params.title: Bernoulli generating function

layout.html: $n$$B_n$numeric
Odd $B_n>1$ are zero. Even $|B_{2k}|$ grows like $2(2k)!/(2\pi)^{2k}$ — the seed of Euler's zeta formula (§3).

blocks.html:
Used in
Power sums, Bernoulli numbers, and Euler-Maclaurin · Euler-Maclaurin summation
Power sums, Bernoulli numbers, and Euler-Maclaurin · Euler's formula for $\zeta(2n)$
Zeta values — ζ at integers · Even values ζ(2n) via Bernoulli numbers
Zeta values — ζ at integers · Negative-integer values and trivial zeros

params.title: Bernoulli generating function

layout.html: $n$$B_n$numeric
Odd $B_n>1$ are zero. Even $|B_{2k}|$ grows like $2(2k)!/(2\pi)^{2k}$ — the seed of Euler's zeta formula (§3).

blocks.html:
3. Euler's formula for $\zeta(2n)$

In 1734 Euler solved the Basel problem by proving $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$. The full even-integer result, in his later notation:

$\displaystyle \zeta(2n) \;=\; (-1)^{n+1}\frac{B_{2n}(2\pi)^{2n}}{2\,(2n)!}.$

The modern derivation: start from the Weierstrass product $\sin(\pi z)=\pi z\prod_{k\ge 1}(1-z^2/k^2)$, take logarithmic derivative, compare with $\pi\cot(\pi z)=\tfrac1z - 2\sum_{n\ge 1}\zeta(2n) z^{2n-1}$, then use the cotangent-to-Bernoulli expansion

$\displaystyle \pi z\cot(\pi z) \;=\; \sum_{n\ge 0}\frac{(-1)^n B_{2n}(2\pi z)^{2n}}{(2n)!}.$

The negative-integer values, by contrast, fall out of the functional equation (§5) as

$\displaystyle \zeta(-n) \;=\; -\frac{B_{n+1}}{n+1}.$

Since $B_3=B_5=B_7=\cdots=0$, this gives $\zeta(-2)=\zeta(-4)=\cdots=0$ — the trivial zeros. And $\zeta(-1)=-B_2/2=-1/12$, the regularised value that makes the string theorist's $1+2+3+\cdots$ finite.

params.title: $\zeta(2n)$ and $\zeta(-n)$ from Bernoulli

blocks.html:
See also
  • Dirichlet series basics

Used in
Power sums, Bernoulli numbers, and Euler-Maclaurin · Functional equation preview via Mellin
Zeta values — ζ at integers · Negative-integer values and trivial zeros

params.title: $\zeta(2n)$ and $\zeta(-n)$ from Bernoulli

blocks.html:
4. Euler-Maclaurin summation

The same Bernoulli numbers that solve $\sum k^m$ also bridge finite sums to integrals of arbitrary smooth functions. Euler-Maclaurin:

$\displaystyle \sum_{k=a}^{b} f(k) \;=\; \int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx \;+\; \frac{f(a)+f(b)}{2} \;+\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{B_{2k}}{(2k)!}\,\bigl[f^{(2k-1)}(b)-f^{(2k-1)}(a)\bigr] \;+\; R_K,$

where $R_K$ is a remainder bounded in terms of $\|f^{(2K+1)}\|_\infty$. Only the even Bernoulli numbers enter the main series (the odd ones, past $B_1$, vanish; $B_1$ is hidden inside the $(f(a)+f(b))/2$ trapezoidal term). Two ways to think about it:

  • Operator calculus. Writing $\Delta=E-1$ where $Ef(x)=f(x+1)$, and $D=d/dx$, Taylor gives $E=e^D$. Then $\Delta=e^D-1$, so $\Delta^{-1}=D^{-1}\cdot\tfrac{D}{e^D-1}=\tfrac{1}{D}\sum B_n D^n/n!$. Applying this operator identity formally to $f$ produces Euler-Maclaurin.
  • Repeated integration by parts. On each unit interval $[k,k+1]$, integrate $f$ against the periodic Bernoulli polynomials $\widetilde B_n(x)=B_n(\{x\})$; the resulting telescoping recovers the series.
It is an asymptotic series, not a convergent one. For most $f$, the error as $K\to\infty$ eventually grows. The trick is to stop near the smallest term; the accuracy is typically $e^{-c\,K}$ for well-chosen $K$.

params.title: Harmonic partial sum $H_n$ via Euler-Maclaurin

layout.html: Green = absolute error of the Euler-Maclaurin estimate for $H_n$; red = error of the naïve $\int_1^n dx/x = \ln n$. The gap is the power of Bernoulli corrections.

blocks.html:
See also
  • Riemann integral

params.title: Harmonic partial sum $H_n$ via Euler-Maclaurin

layout.html: Green = absolute error of the Euler-Maclaurin estimate for $H_n$; red = error of the naïve $\int_1^n dx/x = \ln n$. The gap is the power of Bernoulli corrections.

blocks.html:
5. Functional equation preview

The special values of §3 are not two unrelated facts — they are one fact seen from two sides of a reflection. Define the completed zeta function:

$\displaystyle \xi(s) \;=\; \pi^{-s/2}\,\Gamma(s/2)\,\zeta(s).$

Riemann's 1859 memoir proves

$\displaystyle \xi(s) \;=\; \xi(1-s),$

so $\xi$ is symmetric across the critical line $\operatorname{Re}(s)=\tfrac12$. The proof goes via the theta function $\theta(y)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z} e^{-\pi n^2 y}$ and its Poisson-summation symmetry $\theta(1/y)=\sqrt{y}\,\theta(y)$ — that is why the Mellin transform (see dirichlet-series) is the right bridge.

Pulling $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ apart gives the asymmetric form

$\displaystyle \zeta(1-s) \;=\; 2\,(2\pi)^{-s}\cos(\pi s/2)\,\Gamma(s)\,\zeta(s).$

Plug in $s=2n$: the $\cos(\pi n)=(-1)^n$ factor, together with Euler's $\zeta(2n)=(-1)^{n+1}B_{2n}(2\pi)^{2n}/(2(2n)!)$ and $\Gamma(2n)=(2n-1)!$, collapses to

$\displaystyle \zeta(1-2n) \;=\; -\frac{B_{2n}}{2n}.$

Combined with $\zeta(-2k)=0$ (trivial zeros, from odd $B=0$), this is exactly the reflection of §3 — the Bernoulli numbers are the same numbers on both sides of the mirror.

params.title: $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ consistency check

layout.html: Both columns show $\xi(s)=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$ evaluated at $s$ (right) and $1-s$ (left). They agree; the symmetry point is $s=1/2$ (off-screen, between integers).

blocks.html:
See also
  • Mellin transform and the gamma integral

Used in
Zeta values — ζ at integers · The functional equation ξ(s) = ξ(1−s)

params.title: $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ consistency check

layout.html: Both columns show $\xi(s)=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$ evaluated at $s$ (right) and $1-s$ (left). They agree; the symmetry point is $s=1/2$ (off-screen, between integers).

blocks.html:
6. Application: accurate $\zeta(s)$ near $s=1$

The naïve series $\zeta(s)=\sum_{k\ge 1} k^{-s}$ converges absolutely for $\operatorname{Re}(s)>1$ but agonizingly slowly as $s\to 1^{+}$: the tail $\sum_{k>N} k^{-s}\approx N^{1-s}/(s-1)$. Applying Euler-Maclaurin to the tail gives

$\displaystyle \zeta(s) \;\approx\; \sum_{k=1}^{N-1}\frac{1}{k^{s}} \;+\; \frac{N^{1-s}}{s-1} \;+\; \frac{1}{2 N^{s}} \;+\; \sum_{k=1}^{K}\frac{B_{2k}}{(2k)!}\,\frac{s(s+1)\cdots(s+2k-2)}{N^{s+2k-1}}.$

This is the Riemann-Siegel predecessor: a modest $N$ and $K$ already give 10-digit accuracy uniformly across a reasonable strip. The widget below compares the two strategies at any $s>1$; notice how the EM-corrected estimate blows past the naïve partial sum by many orders of magnitude for the same number of terms.

params.title: Naïve vs Euler-Maclaurin ζ(s)
params.hint: same $N$; EM wins by orders of magnitude

params.title: Naïve vs Euler-Maclaurin ζ(s)
params.hint: same $N$; EM wins by orders of magnitude

blocks.html:
Where this goes next. The same Euler-Maclaurin machinery powers Riemann-Siegel, the sharpest elementary algorithm for $\zeta(s)$ on the critical line; the even-Bernoulli corrections become a trigonometric series in $t=\operatorname{Im}(s)$, and the asymptotic cutoff is chosen around $K\sim \sqrt{t/(2\pi)}$. That is the backbone of every numerical zero-verification ever computed.
Part of the Interactive Mathematics Notebook · open in a browser, no server needed
